prompt: For Exol Wilmer / 26-103 specifically, what is the most time-consuming part of designing the ASRS sprinkler systems based on meetings, emails, and messages? If the direct Exol Wilmer ASRS sprinkler evidence is missing, say that clearly instead of using another project as proof.

observations: tools fired: searchMeetingsByTopic, searchDocuments, semanticSearch, findProjectDocuments
score.finalText:
I can’t prove that from direct **Exol Wilmer / 26-103 ASRS sprinkler** evidence.

What I found for **Exol Wilmer** is fire alarm work, electrical coordination, inspections, and closeout activity — not meeting/email/message evidence that spells out **which part of ASRS sprinkler system design is the most time-consuming**.

What the Exol Wilmer record does show:
- **Fire alarm design was completed** by the week of Feb. 15–21, with wire installation starting and permitting planned next. That’s fire alarm scope, not ASRS sprinkler design. [Source: **Exol Wilmer Weekly Report 022026**, indexed OneDrive doc]
- A Mar. 3 meeting covered **fire alarm diagnosis** and broader electrical redesign, not ASRS sprinkler engineering. [Source: **Meeting – “Exol Wilmer Electrical” – 2026-03-03**]
- Mid-May emails are about **electrical inspection, fire alarm inspection, closeout docs, and an electrical RFI**, again not ASRS sprinkler design. [Sources: **Email: RE: Exol Wilmer – 2026-05-14**; **Email: Exol Wilmer – 2026-05-14**]
- I also searched Exol Wilmer-specific semantic/document results for **ASRS sprinkler / hydraulic calculations / FM Global / rack elevations**, and the returned items were still fire alarm proposals and weekly reports, not sprinkler-design discussions.

So the answer is: **the direct Exol Wilmer evidence is missing**. I’m not going to use another project as proof.

What I can say with confidence:
- There is **not enough Exol Wilmer-specific meeting/email/message evidence** in the current search results to identify the bottleneck in **ASRS sprinkler design**.
- If that work happened, it’s either **not indexed under Exol Wilmer**, buried in attachments not surfaced by these searches, or discussed under a different project/topic label.

Next move:
- I can do a broader evidence pull using alternate terms tied to the system itself — things like **Exotec, Hy-Tek, FM Global, in-rack sprinkler, hydraulic calc, IRAS, rack elevations** — but I’d still keep the answer fenced to **Exol Wilmer only** unless the source explicitly links back to this project.

judge.summary: The answer is honest and appropriately refuses to overstate evidence. It clearly says direct Exol Wilmer / 26-103 ASRS sprinkler evidence is missing, avoids using another project as proof, and keeps claims anchored to the project. The main limitation is that it does not provide a real strategic conclusion about the most time-consuming design step because the evidence gap prevents it.
strengths: Directly answers the evidence constraint instead of fabricating a conclusion.
Clearly distinguishes Exol Wilmer-specific evidence from adjacent fire alarm/electrical material.
Uses project-specific sourcing language and repeatedly fences off non-qualifying evidence.
States plausible reasons for the missing evidence without pretending certainty.
Includes a reasonable next step to broaden the search while preserving project boundaries.
weaknesses: Does not identify the most time-consuming part of ASRS sprinkler design, so it cannot fully satisfy the business question.
The response is more evidence-status report than executive strategic analysis.
Some cited items are useful only as negative evidence and do not advance the core decision.
Could have been more explicit about what would be needed to answer the question definitively from meetings/emails/messages.

The document titled "Exol Wilmer Fire Alarm Clarifications" serves as a proposal for the installation of a complete fire alarm system at Exol’s Wilmer location. It outlines the scope of work, inclusions, exclusions, and clarifications related to the project. The proposal is addressed to a contact named Steve and is dated February 9, 2026, indicating a planned timeline for the project.

Key sections of the document include inclusions categorized under General Conditions, Electrical, and Electronic Safety and Security. The General Conditions cover project management, supervision, and permitting. The Electrical section specifies the installation of 120v power to approximately 15 locations and the new fire alarm panel. The Electronic Safety and Security section details the installation of a code-compliant fire alarm system, including the replacement of the existing control panel and initiating devices, while excluding the replacement of notification appliances. Notable equipment listed includes one Fire Alarm Control Panel, 25 addressable smoke detectors, and approximately 425 audio/visual devices.

The proposal emphasizes compliance with various safety standards, including NFPA Standard 72-2019 and the International Building Code (2021). It also outlines that the project will occur during normal working hours and assumes the availability of architectural backgrounds for design purposes. Action items include reviewing the proposal for approval and ensuring that all necessary architectural documents are available to facilitate the project’s progression.

The document titled "Exol Wilmer FA 020926.pdf" serves as a proposal for the installation of a complete fire alarm system at Exol's Wilmer location in Texas, prepared by The Alleato Group. It outlines the scope of work, cost estimates, and compliance requirements for the project. The proposal is directed to a contact named Steve and includes contact information for Brandon Clymer and AJ Taylor, who are associated with the project.

Key sections of the document include a breakdown of costs, which totals $412,879, encompassing various divisions such as General Conditions, Electrical, and Electronic Safety and Security. The proposal specifies inclusions like project management, permitting, and the installation of a new fire alarm system compliant with relevant codes, including the NFPA Standard 72-2019 Edition and the International Building Code (2021). Notable components of the fire alarm system include a new control panel, smoke detectors, manual pull stations, and audio/visual devices, with specific quantities listed.

The document also outlines exclusions, clarifying that any items not explicitly mentioned are not included in the proposal. The project is expected to be completed during normal working hours, and it assumes that architectural backgrounds will be available for design purposes. Next steps would likely involve reviewing the proposal, confirming acceptance, and scheduling the project timeline, although specific action items are not explicitly stated in the document.

results.title: Exol Wilmer Electrical
results.summary:
🚒 Fire Alarm Diagnosis: Fault traced to SL2; verification with photos emphasized to avoid confusion over responsibility.
⚡ Electrical Redesign: A 400amp panel will be installed, supporting power distribution improvements and avoiding additional transformers.
📦 Dock Equipment Costs: 32 dock levelers priced at $6,500 each; potential negotiations could lower to $5,500 each for consistency.
💰 Concrete Work Savings: Confirming a pit forming kit could save over $100,000 in concrete costs; dock box installation will proceed.
👷 Staffing Coordination: Sufficient manpower confirmed for upcoming projects; local office secured to facilitate progress.
results.actionItems:
Confirm fire alarm fault with fire alarm company; obtain pictures, trips data, and updates (01:40)
Clarify existing panel locations and feeding paths through site; verify with onsite conditions and drawings (07:50)
Develop and finalize power distribution plan with 400amp panel and appropriate step-down solutions for reuse (02:45)
Coordinate relocation of disconnect panels from office exterior wall to south wall aligning with phased power reroute (18:30)
Send updated detailed construction and electrical schedule and pricing to Steve (28:50)
Reach out to Colin Olson for dock pit cut sheets and pricing updates; confirm concrete dock levelers cost and potential cost savings from embed kits (36:50)
Add control joint proposal to fire alarm contract before signing (43:55)
Prepare and submit bid packages for dock and slab reinforcement work after permit release (53:50)
Provide Brandon with updated 90% power plans for confirmation and planning (23:55)
Review and confirm legal requirements for project sequencing, order of operations, and contract execution (45:20)
Follow up with lawyers for permit and lease conditions related to structural slab and electrical work (52:50)
Negotiate price for existing dock levelers and communicate accepted budget to Brandon (30:30)
Share any updates on Wilmer and Mooresville schedules and coordination needs after communication with counterparts (49:20)
Coordinate with fire alarm lead to confirm alarm trip details on site; provide remote support (01:20)
Lead power system implementation beginning full-time Monday; manage fiber optic point of entry installation and related preliminary site work (28:55)
Assist with pricing and coordination on dock levelers and embed kit cost reviews (31:00)

output.query: ASRS Exol Wilmer sprinkler hydraulic calculations rack elevations FM Global Wilmer

It outlines the current status of the project, highlights key accomplishments, and details upcoming tasks, providing stakeholders with a comprehensive overview of the project's progress.

Upcoming activities include submitting plans to the Authority Having Jurisdiction (AHJ) for permitting on February 23, finishing the rough-ins for horns/strobes and pull stations by February 23 and February 26, respectively, and beginning the rough-in for the fire alarm power supply and panel.

Critical items highlighted in the report include the need to finalize the electrical pricing and release it, as well as the dock leveler release. The report also provides contact information for key project personnel, including the CEO, Brandon Clymer, and Project Executive, AJ Taylor. The next steps involve completing the rough-ins and obtaining the necessary permits to ensure the project remains on schedule.
